<!-- prettier-ignore-start -->

# TS9006

**Category:** Typeclass Resolution · **Severity:** Warning

## Message

```
Duplicate instance {typeclass}<{type}> registered
```

## Explanation

Two or more instances of {typeclass}&lt;{type}&gt; have been registered.

This creates ambiguity in instance resolution. The transformer will use
the first registered instance, but this may not be the intended behavior.

To fix:
1. Remove duplicate @instance declarations
2. Or use explicit instance selection with summon()

<!-- prettier-ignore-end -->
<!-- generated:end -->

## Example

```typescript
import type { Eq } from "@typesugar/std";

interface Point {
  x: number;
  y: number;
}

/** @impl Eq<Point> */
const eqPoint: Eq<Point> = {
  equals: (a, b) => a.x === b.x && a.y === b.y,
};

/** @impl Eq<Point> */
// warning: TS9006 — Duplicate instance Eq<Point> registered
const eqPointByX: Eq<Point> = {
  equals: (a, b) => a.x === b.x,
};
```

## How to fix

Keep exactly one registered instance per `(typeclass, type)` pair — resolution uses the first one, so the duplicate is silently ignored:

```typescript
/** @impl Eq<Point> */
const eqPoint: Eq<Point> = {
  equals: (a, b) => a.x === b.x && a.y === b.y,
};
```

If you genuinely need a second behavior, don't register it: keep it as a plain `const eqPointByX: Eq<Point>` (no `@impl` tag) and pass it explicitly where needed, or wrap `Point` in a newtype and register the alternative instance for the wrapper.
